chunks.title: Introduction to Chronic Cardio
chunks.summary: The discussion begins with the speaker sharing personal experiences with chronic cardio and its ineffectiveness. They emphasize the misconception of needing to engage in excessive cardio for weight management and fitness.
chunks.transcript: I mean I I love that we're talking about chronic cardio um I I used to believe when I weighed that 300 pounds just even in my late teens early 20s I've got a Cannondale road bike specialized mountain bike I would put in 20 miles a day and I would do lots of cardio in Albuquerque and it just doesn't work uh and I I don't think I did myself any favors with that and now with smarter not harder like okay using AI Technologies and all that you can get six times better cardiovascular response from five minutes three times a week of cardio using the algorithms I'm talking about in the book compared to an hour a day five days a week so you probably spend five minutes a day brushing your teeth or sorry five minutes or 15 minutes a week brushing your teeth so you can get six times more cardio in the amount of time he's been brushing your teeth than you can from doing an hour a day and there's university studies backing this up and like that's one of the dozens of different things I'm talking

chunks.title: Rethinking Exercise Efficiency
chunks.summary: The speaker introduces a more efficient approach to cardio, suggesting that short bursts of workout can yield better cardiovascular benefits. They provide evidence from studies and promote the philosophy of 'working smarter, not harder'.
chunks.transcript: about in the book it's like look I'm too busy to spend an hour a day doing cardio right and it doesn't work but we have this algorithm in our minds that says oh if if we work really hard we'll get results and the reality do you have some friends who work really hard and they don't get results Paul I know some people like that there's no correlation between working hard and getting results there are people who do barely any work and they just magically get great results there are some people who work really hard and never see any results so maybe doing what works is better than working hard just saying yeah and I think I mean that you kind of captured that in the title of your book

chunks.title: Personal Reflections on Overtraining
chunks.summary: The speaker reflects on their personal history of overtraining in cycling and running, explaining how it affected their health and lifestyle. They describe the negative impacts of excessive cardio, including fatigue and a sedentary post-workout lifestyle.
chunks.transcript: like you know work smarter not harder be intentional about what you're doing I mean I'm a victim of this or I should say I was I was wrong in my estimation of this in the past and I ran tons and tons of miles in the past and I now I Sprint uh as I'm paddling out of the ocean I Sprint back to my car and that's you know I'll Sprint paddle in the ocean when I'm trying not to get killed by a wave or trying to catch a wave and that's going back to the car but that's all the cardio I do and you know that that's what and I feel much better doing that I remember my days of I was a cyclist so I over trained on the bike um I'm very thankful that my perineal artery still works um after sitting on the bike seat for that much time I know right right yeah it's a useful artery it's a useful that's a big important artery especially for men but for women too and I over trained I remember being so over trained as a cyclist so over trained as a uh as a runner that I would just when I wasn't running all I would want to do was lay on the couch and watch movies and do like nothing productive with my life could happen I had a girlfriend when I lived in Boulder Colorado and I wouldn't even go for walks with her because I didn't want to be on my feet because I was so over trained from riding my bike three to four hours a day and it was just all I could do when I would do distance running when I used to be an ultra Runner I would go out on 20 mile runs on Sunday mornings and run for three or four hours and in the mountains um and you know I come back and I would eat a meal and watch stupid movies this is my life what was I doing and look if you guys are listening to this and you really want to be an ultra Runner great just understand that it's maybe not so good for your life and your hormones and other productivity in your life there

chunks.title: The Psychological Aspect of Chronic Cardio
chunks.summary: The conversation shifts to the psychological dependence on chronic cardio and how it may indicate deeper emotional issues. The speaker discusses the stress and anxiety that can lead individuals to feel they must engage in prolonged workout sessions.
chunks.transcript: are better ways to get cardiovascular fitness if that's what you desire that's the thing and I I have some friends who are like yeah but if I don't you know do my chronic cardio for an hour a day I just get really stressed and anxious I'm like okay um there's a word for that it's called addiction and it's driven by opiates and it's driven by the fact you're probably burning off excess adrenaline or excess cortisol because you're stressed and you can be stressed because you have a nutrient deficiency problem and you can be stressed because you're over training or you can be stressed because you have a mean boss actually versus you just think your bosses mean because you're stressed in general from your body and if you need to do chronic cardio every day to feel sane you have an emotional problem if you just love it okay I know addicts and and this may trigger some people especially former addicts but I'm just going to say it they're like but I like my drugs and I don't want to give them up okay that is a choice and it is okay to make your choice and you might not be making it from a place of clear thinking and clear-headedness because you're on drugs but it is your right to make that choice

chunks.title: Consequences of Excessive Exercise
chunks.summary: The speaker warns about the long-term consequences of engaging in chronic cardio, equating it to self-harm and emphasizing its detriment to overall health and longevity. They advocate for more balanced and enjoyable forms of exercise.
chunks.transcript: and people who've been through a recovery process also know that until you hit rock bottom as an addict it's oftentimes hard to see the need to go into recovery so I'm just going to say it if you are beating your body senseless over time you're experiencing the stress fractures you're doing all these things and you're doing it because you think it's moral you think it's good or because you get a high from the endorphins from it it's not a longevity strategy at all it's not good for you and I will go so far as to say you could call it a form of self-harm and I I have done it I I did it because I believed it would work and it did not work and as an anti-aging expert um I I don't recommend it you need to move for 20 minutes a day you need to do stuff you love but if what you love is you know grinding out you know 20 mile runs at three times a week it's not
